petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented researcher in the sciences, specifically working in computational/theoretical biophysics with a focus on protein folding and aggregation (including research related to the Green Fluorescent Protein and Alzheimer's disease/AP peptide research). He has been employed as a postdoctoral research associate at a university since 2006. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, with a priority date of October 26, 2010.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence regarding lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards for excellence in the field, though the specific awards are not detailed in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director discussed this criterion and found that the petitioner failed to establish his eligibility. On appeal, the petitioner did not contest the director's findings for this criterion or offer additional arguments. The AAO therefore considered this issue abandoned, citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226, 1228 n. 2 (11th Cir. 2005) and Hristov v. Roark.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of published material about him or his work, though the specific publications are not detailed for this criterion. Note that magazine reports related to his research were submitted but the petitioner requested they be considered under the original contributions criterion instead.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director discussed this criterion and found that the petitioner failed to establish his eligibility. On appeal, the petitioner did not contest the director's findings for this criterion or offer additional arguments. The AAO therefore considered this issue abandoned, citing Sepulveda and Hristov.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's participation as a judge of the work of others in the same or an allied field (specific details not elaborated in the decision).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner met the requirements of this criterion. The AAO affirmed the director's favorable determination as it relates to this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a Web of Knowledge printout reflecting 98 citations relating to 14 works (with self-citations omitted) with the initial filing; (2) additional citation evidence in response to the RFE, though the majority postdated the priority date; (3) magazine reports about his research including coverage in two issues of a publication where the Deputy Assistant Managing Editor indicated the journal highlighted his work 'because it was newsworthy, scientifically significant, and societally important' and that the research 'could have implications for drug development'; (4) coverage in Faculty of 1000; (5) multiple expert letters including from a Professor and Dean who stated the petitioner's 'pioneering contributions' included developing a computational model that 'played a crucial role in interpreting the experimental folding data to unravel the folding mechanism' of the Green Fluorescent Protein (for which the Nobel Prize was awarded in 2008); (6) a letter from an Assistant Professor in the Department of Chemistry calling the findings 'path changing' and 'groundbreaking'; (7) a letter from a colleague stating the petitioner 'made extraordinary discoveries, which had tremendous impact on the field' related to designing drugs to slow protein aggregation (this author had spent 13 months as a visiting professor at the same institution between 2000-2011); (8) a letter from an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics referencing therapeutic strategies to combat Alzheimer's disease through drugs targeting the AP peptide; and (9) additional expert letters.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence insufficient. First, much of the citation evidence postdated the priority date and could not be considered per Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45. Only three of the petitioner's articles had garnered moderate citation according to Web of Knowledge, and the citations generally provided background information rather than showing reliance on the petitioner's findings. The AAO noted moderate citation is not an automatic indicator of major significance. Regarding the magazine coverage, the statement that research 'could have implications for drug development' indicated future rather than present impact. One article only mentioned the petitioner while another discussed a project he assisted but didn't mention him. The AAO emphasized that published material criterion and original contributions criterion are separate and distinct, citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin. Regarding the expert letters: the Professor/Dean did not describe measurable impact from the 'valuable insights' and did not link the petitioner to the Nobel Prize; the Chemistry Assistant Professor offered no examples of drug companies adopting the petitioner's methodology; the colleague's letter did not demonstrate impact beyond the petitioner's immediate circle of colleagues, especially given she had been a visiting professor at the same institution; the Physics Assistant Professor only speculated about future applications. The AAO noted that letters speculating about future benefits do not establish present impact as required. Per Matter of Caron International and Matter of V-K-, expert letters are advisory and not presumptive evidence; they are most persuasive when supported by independent evidence. The AAO concluded that not every researcher performing original research has made a contribution of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's authorship of scholarly articles in professional or major trade publications (14 works referenced via the Web of Knowledge printout).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner met the requirements of this criterion. The AAO affirmed the director's favorable determination as it relates to this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only two of the ten regulatory criteria (judging and scholarly articles), failing to meet the antecedent regulatory requirement of qualifying evidence under at least three criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Because the petitioner did not clear this initial evidentiary threshold, the AAO declined to conduct a final merits determination under the Kazarian two-step framework. The AAO noted that while it concluded the evidence was not indicative of a level of expertise consistent with the small percentage at the very top of the field or of sustained national or international acclaim, it did not need to explain that conclusion in a final merits determination. The burden of establishing eligibility rested on the petitioner, and that burden was not met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I&N Dec. 45 (Reg'l Comm'r 1971)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190
fraud_or_procedural_issues: No fraud or misrepresentation findings. Procedural notes: (1) The petitioner did not contest the director's findings on the awards and published material criteria on appeal, so the AAO deemed those issues abandoned under Sepulveda and Hristov. (2) Much of the citation evidence submitted in response to the RFE postdated the October 26, 2010 priority date and could not be considered for establishing eligibility at the time of filing, per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1),(12) and Matter of Katigbak. (3) The petitioner cited unpublished AAO decisions, which the AAO noted are not binding under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(c). (4) The petitioner requested that magazine reports be considered under the original contributions criterion rather than the published material criterion where the director had considered them.
